title: The Disproportionate-Assets Case: A Continuing Story! (5)
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. Part 5 deliberately reprints the preceding Bhavani Singh chronology on a distinct set of source pages; that source repetition is preserved rather than treated as duplication. The translation retains the attributed judicial order, political allegations, expert observations, exact dates and amounts, rhetorical questions, and “To be continued” ending. The Tamil below is a source-checked derived reading copy in which only clear OCR character, numeral, spacing, and word-break errors are repaired. The canonical source remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Even after the trial has been deliberately delayed for more than fifteen years, various devices and tactics are being used to postpone it still further. One such device was a petition filed by the Public Prosecutor himself. He asked the judge to have 1,116 kilograms of silver articles belonging to Jayalalithaa, which had been entrusted to a man named Bhaskaran in Chennai, brought to the Bengaluru court.

When the case was about to end, why did the Public Prosecutor himself need to come forward with such a petition? In my view, it was filed with the ulterior motive of continuing to delay the case. A petition on behalf of the Professor, however, informed the court that the particular Bhaskaran had already died. Special Judge John Michael D'Cunha therefore dismissed Public Prosecutor Bhavani Singh's petition in a judgment delivered on 28 February 2014. In that judgment he said:

“This case has been proceeding in Bengaluru for more than nine years. When Judge B.A. Mallikarjunaiah heard it, the accused appeared in person and gave their statements under Section 313 of the Code of Criminal Procedure. When Judge Balakrishna heard it, examination of witnesses was completed. Counsel for both the prosecution and the accused completed their arguments.

“On 14 August 2013 itself, Judge Balakrishna recorded that every matter connected with the case had been completed. The case has now gone beyond that point and reached its final stage. For the past thirteen years, the prosecution took no steps to obtain these silver articles belonging to Jayalalithaa's side from Bhaskaran and produce them before the court.

“When, on a petition filed by a third party in the case, the General Secretary of the DMK, the gold and silver ornaments in Chennai were brought to Bengaluru, why did Public Prosecutor Bhavani Singh not file a petition seeking these silver articles from Bhaskaran?
“Bhavani Singh has served as Public Prosecutor since February 2013. During that period he filed no such petition.

“Did Bhavani Singh, appointed Public Prosecutor in this case, not know that the first accused's silver articles were in Chennai? His claim that he learned of them only when reading the documents for final arguments is neither truthful nor credible. He knew that Bhaskaran, who had taken the silver articles, was no longer alive. Even after Bhaskaran's death was established before the court with a death certificate, the Public Prosecutor refused to accept it, persisted in his position, and continued pressing the petition. That is not legally acceptable.

“This gives rise to the view that Public Prosecutor Bhavani Singh acted with the intention of delaying the case. All the silver articles in the first accused's home had been valued, and only their monetary value was included. The case records contain the relevant details. When the trial is ending, there is no need to waste time bringing the silver articles here. That argument too is unsound. The Public Prosecutor's petition seeking their production therefore has no basis. I regard it as unnecessary and dismiss it.”

If this is what the Special Judge himself recorded at length in his order on Bhavani Singh's petition, do we need any further example to understand how the Public Prosecutor has been acting in close sympathy with the accused?

Jayalalithaa's side, the accused, went all the way to the Supreme Court insisting that a lawyer such as Bhavani Singh must continue as Public Prosecutor. Is the true meaning and ulterior motive behind their insistence now as plain to everyone as a gooseberry in the palm, or not?

In the disproportionate-assets case, Jayalalithaa has two lawyers. One is Bhavani Singh, paid by the Government, who disregards the Government's own prosecution, fails in the duty he must perform, and argues in support of the accused Jayalalithaa. The other is her own lawyer, B. Kumar, paid by Jayalalithaa to argue for her. Even if Tamil Nadu's newspapers try to conceal this shameful truth, something never before seen or heard in judicial history, can it remain unknown to Tamil Nadu's discerning people?

Judge John Michael D'Cunha announced that final arguments would begin on 7 March 2014. But the Public Prosecutor did not appear in court that day. The case was adjourned to the tenth, and Bhavani Singh failed to appear then too. The matter next came up on 14 March, when the judge ordered final arguments to begin.

Bhavani Singh appeared that day, claimed he was unwell, sought ten days' time in continuation of his efforts to delay the trial, and submitted medical certificates. Dissatisfied with them, the judge ordered Bhavani Singh, who had attended court but refused to argue, to pay a fine equal to one day's fee, Rs 65,000.

On the fifteenth, Bhavani Singh again failed to appear without notice or explanation. His assistant Murugesh said he knew nothing about the Public Prosecutor's absence. The judge therefore imposed another fine of Rs 65,000, his fee for that day.

The lawyers appearing for Jayalalithaa said they could begin their arguments only after the Public Prosecutor completed his. The judge then firmly ordered the Public Prosecutor to begin on 21 March 2014.

Meanwhile, Bhavani Singh petitioned the Karnataka High Court to cancel the Special Court's fine for his non-appearance. Karnataka High Court Justice Sathyanarayana Rao dismissed the petition. Bhavani Singh therefore appeared before the Special Court on 21 March and began his final arguments, compiling and reading what the witnesses in the case had testified about Jayalalithaa accumulating assets worth many crores of rupees. Only then did the world begin to learn those details one by one.

During my election tours in April, I spoke about the truths those witnesses had stated concerning the disproportionate-assets case, and a few newspapers began publishing them. That was enough! Can those who have done wrong bear it? They began struggling in every possible way to devise some means of securing another adjournment and delaying the trial.

One such course began after the Public Prosecutor appointed by the Government to argue against Jayalalithaa was accused of neglecting his duty and removed by the Karnataka Government itself. Jayalalithaa's side went to the Supreme Court insisting that this same Bhavani Singh must continue. Justice Chauhan accepted the request and ordered that he could do so.

When Bhavani Singh repeatedly failed to appear before the Special Court, the Special Judge fined him. He appealed to the Karnataka High Court, where his petition was dismissed. Bhavani Singh then took the matter to the Supreme Court.
Why do the accused want, and strive to retain, a particular lawyer appointed by the Government to argue against them?
Why does that particular lawyer go all the way to the Supreme Court and ask that this case be stayed for another three weeks?
If Bhavani Singh is truly unwell, could he not resign? Or could the trial not proceed through his assistant? Once his health improves, could he not return and continue the proceedings?

After the Supreme Court ordered in 2003 that the case be completed quickly and heard daily, why must the Supreme Court itself now act contrary to that direction and stay the trial for three weeks? Those three weeks fell at a crucial point. If the case proceeded and judgment were delivered during that period, perhaps it could affect the accused in the parliamentary election. Has this “drama of adjournment” therefore occurred in a manner that makes impartial observers suspect precisely that? What is the secret behind it?

If an entire trial can be postponed for three weeks because one lawyer is unwell, what will happen if every lawyer in the country obtains a medical certificate to secure an adjournment and seeks a stay of three weeks or three months?

The Supreme Court Bench that made this order comprised Justices B.S. Chauhan and J. Chelameswar. We cannot forget that Justice Chauhan was due to retire shortly. He was also the judge who, only weeks earlier, had ordered that Bhavani Singh could continue as Public Prosecutor. Bhavani Singh's principal request concerned the fine. But if the Supreme Court stayed the Special Court trial itself for three weeks, can one not see the remarkable generosity with which the Supreme Court acted?

The Special Court's fine on Bhavani Singh for two days was Rs 1.20 lakh. Do you know how much he spent through litigation to avoid paying it? Senior advocates appearing in the Supreme Court charge several lakhs for a case. Nagendra Rai, who argued there for Bhavani Singh, is a senior advocate and would have been paid several lakhs. Filing a petition in the Supreme Court itself costs Rs 2 lakh. Would anyone spend several lakhs to save Rs 1.20 lakh?

Judging by Bhavani Singh's finances, he did not appear wealthy enough, as a Public Prosecutor, to pay fees running into lakhs. Is it not clear that someone else used him as a pawn, expected something in return, spent money for him in the Supreme Court and engaged a senior advocate?

What benefit did those behind him expect? Who believed that Bhavani Singh's continuation and his arguments would greatly serve their interests? A further three-week adjournment enabled them to prevent judgment before the parliamentary general election. If they feared that a judgment might affect the election result, did that not mean they carried a burden in their lap? Did the three-week adjournment not mean that they wanted judgment postponed until after the election?

I am not alone in saying this. Economist and legal expert Dr K. Sridhar said: “Ordinarily it is the accused who seek adjournments. In this case, the prosecution seeking an adjournment is not proper procedure. One thing is clear: Bhavani Singh did not approach the Supreme Court at his own expense.”

Senior advocate Suresh Nayak similarly said: “Legal experts are watching how the person arguing for the prosecution in this case, Bhavani Singh, is functioning. There is no legal expert or politician who has failed to observe the course of a case that has challenged the legal system for the past seventeen years. Bhavani Singh had no need to go all the way to the Supreme Court against the Special Court's action in fining him. Nor is he in a position to spend several lakhs to cancel a small fine. What is clear is that certain political forces are using him for their own interests.”

Somehow, they secured a Supreme Court stay preventing the trial from proceeding for three weeks. The people understood very well that neither the Public Prosecutor's health nor the fine imposed on him was the reason for that stay. They understood the real reason too. Tamil Nadu's people can easily understand who is trying to divert the case through all these measures, and with what purpose.

Justice Lodha, who succeeded Justice Sathasivam of Tamil Nadu as Chief Justice of India, delivered an important judgment on 10 March. He ruled: “In cases against serving Members of Parliament and Members of Legislative Assemblies, courts must complete the trial within one year of the case being instituted. If a court cannot complete it within a year, it must explain the reason to the Chief Justice of the High Court concerned.”

We saw both that judgment and the provision in the Prevention of Corruption Act that trials under the Act must not be stayed. Yet, contrary to both, the Supreme Court itself stayed Jayalalithaa's disproportionate-assets trial for three weeks.

During the hearing of Bhavani Singh's appeal seeking cancellation of the fine, the judges asked his counsel why he should not pay the fine imposed for taking excessive adjournments. They reduced it to Rs 20,000 but directed that it must be paid. They also ordered him to appear without fail before the Bengaluru Special Court and not offer excuses such as the death of his father or mother. The Supreme Court then lifted the three-week stay.
(To be continued)
With affection,
M.K.
